skill.name: woocommerce-backend-dev
skill.description: Guides WooCommerce backend PHP development using project conventions for classes, hooks, dependency injection, data integrity, and unit tests.
skill.summary: Create WooCommerce backend PHP code and tests that follow project conventions.
skill.author: woocommerce

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command and weak cryptography patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown code fences, PHP examples, version annotations, and documentation links. One real external command instruction exists for using git history to document hooks, which is legitimate development guidance but should be treated as medium risk because hook names can come from user or repository content. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, malicious network behavior, or real cryptographic API usage was found.

medium_findings.title: Repository Command Guidance Requires Review
medium_findings.description: The hook documentation instructs the assistant to use git log with a hook-name search string. This is a normal repository-history task, but hook names should be treated as untrusted text and passed as arguments, not interpolated into a shell command.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command instruction is explicit and legitimate for development. Confidence is high enough to warn because unsafe shell interpolation of hook names could create command-injection risk in an agent workflow.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Commands
low_findings.description: Most external command alerts point to PHP code blocks and inline Markdown examples. These blocks describe WooCommerce coding patterns and do not execute commands by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown PHP examples, not Ruby backtick execution. The pattern appears to be a static-analysis parser false positive.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts do not correspond to hashing, encryption, or random-number APIs in the reviewed files. The cited content is documentation prose, version guidance, paths, or examples unrelated to cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no MD5, SHA-1, weak cipher, or insecure randomness usage at the cited locations. The scanner likely matched unrelated Markdown text or version-like strings.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The network findings are links to WordPress, WooCommerce, and PSR-4 documentation. They do not instruct the assistant to fetch data or send repository, credential, or user information to external services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation references with no network request code around them. This supports a false-positive assessment for network exfiltration risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Git Command in Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill tells the assistant to use git history search when documenting existing hooks. This should be executed only with safe argument handling and a trusted repository context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The external command pattern is present and intentional. It is not malicious, but it can become risky if hook names are placed directly into a shell string.

content.value_statement: WooCommerce backend changes must follow many local PHP, hook, dependency injection, and test conventions. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused guidance for creating compliant backend code and unit tests.
actual_capabilities: Guides new WooCommerce classes into src/Internal or public src locations.
Defines method, variable, hook, and class naming conventions for backend PHP.
Explains docblock, @since, @internal, and PHPStan annotation requirements.
Shows dependency injection patterns for WooCommerce container-managed services.
Provides hook callback and hook documentation conventions.
Supplies unit test structure, naming, logger mocking, and data integrity guidance.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not run tests, linting, or static analysis by itself.
It assumes the target project follows WooCommerce trunk conventions.
It does not cover frontend, REST API, or release workflow topics in depth.
It includes examples that must be adapted to the actual class and repository context.

use_cases.title: Add Backend Classes
use_cases.description: Create new WooCommerce PHP classes with correct namespaces, file locations, visibility, and dependency injection patterns.

use_cases.title: Document Hooks Correctly
use_cases.description: Add or update actions and filters with consistent callback names, parameter documentation, and @since annotations.

use_cases.title: Write Unit Tests
use_cases.description: Create WooCommerce unit tests with project naming, @testdox comments, fixtures, assertions, and logger mocks.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Backend Class
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new backend feature class.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the woocommerce-backend-dev skill. Create a WooCommerce backend class for [feature] and explain the file location, namespace, constructor or init method, and tests needed.

prompt_templates.title: Modify Existing PHP Code
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for a focused backend change.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the woocommerce-backend-dev skill. Modify [class or method] to [behavior]. Follow WooCommerce naming, visibility, docblock, PHPStan, and data integrity conventions.

prompt_templates.title: Add or Update Hooks
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request involving WordPress actions or filters.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the woocommerce-backend-dev skill. Add or update the hook for [event]. Include callback naming, parameters, @internal usage, and correct @since guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Design Tests for a Risky Change
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for data-sensitive backend work.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the woocommerce-backend-dev skill before writing tests. Design unit tests for [change], including ownership checks, invalid states, race-condition concerns, and logger mocking if needed.

output_examples.input: Create a backend class that validates draft order cleanup.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies the internal class location, proposes private helper methods by default, adds concise docblock requirements, and lists unit tests for valid drafts, non-draft orders, and missing orders.

output_examples.input: Add documentation for an existing WooCommerce action hook.
output_examples.output: The assistant names the callback consistently, lists required parameter documentation, explains how to determine the @since value, and warns to run repository history commands safely.

output_examples.input: Write tests for code that logs invalid payment suggestion data.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines a WC_Unit_Test_Case test file, uses a system-under-test property, describes a fake logger, and includes cleanup expectations for filters.

best_practices: Invoke this skill before writing WooCommerce PHP unit tests or backend code changes.
Treat hook names, IDs, and request data as untrusted until validated or escaped.
Run the project test and lint commands after applying generated changes.
anti_patterns: Do not add standalone functions when a class method fits the WooCommerce architecture.
Do not hard delete or modify order data without state, ownership, and capability checks.
Do not copy examples without adapting namespaces, @since values, and test paths.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps create and modify WooCommerce backend PHP code, hooks, dependency injection patterns, PHPDoc, and unit tests.

faq.question: Does it run WooCommerce tests?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance for writing compliant tests, but the user or agent must run the repository test commands.

faq.question: Can it create frontend code?
faq.answer: It is focused on backend PHP. Frontend work should use a separate WooCommerce or WordPress frontend skill.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes legitimate git command guidance for hook history. Commands should be run with safe argument handling.

faq.question: Are the documentation links risky?
faq.answer: The links point to public WordPress, WooCommerce, and PSR documentation. They do not send data or execute code.

faq.question: When should this skill be used?
faq.answer: Use it before adding WooCommerce backend classes, modifying PHP logic, adding hooks, or writing PHP unit tests.
